---
format:
  docx:
    reference-doc: ./references/style.docx
    highlight-style: github
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: ./references/references.bib
csl: ./references/apa.csl
---

# Preface {.unnumbered}

This book is a practical, comprehensive introduction to geospatial data science with R. It teaches the foundations every analyst of spatial data must understand, how spatial data are represented, referenced, measured, combined, visualised, and modelled, using free and open tools. It is written for scientists, analysts, and auditors who need to move confidently from a stack of shapefiles and satellite imagery to a defensible result, and it assumes some statistics but no prior GIS.

The premise is that spatial analysis should be reproducible: you should be able to open a dataset, understand its geometry and coordinate system, join it to other data, summarise it, map it, and quantify the uncertainty, all in a single script that another person can re-run. This course builds that capability from the ground up, one concept at a time.

## Learning outcomes {.unnumbered}

By the end of the course you will be able to:

1. Set up a reproducible R project for spatial work
2. Represent vector data with the simple-features standard and its geometry types
3. Understand coordinate reference systems, projections, and spherical geometry
4. Reason about attributes, their support, and correct spatial aggregation
5. Wrangle vector data with `sf` and the `tidyverse`
6. Perform spatial operations: predicates, overlays, buffers, and joins
7. Read, process, and align raster data with `terra`
8. Extract raster values to points and work with spatiotemporal data cubes
9. Produce publication-quality static and interactive maps
10. Analyse point patterns and interpolate surfaces with geostatistics
11. Measure spatial autocorrelation and fit spatial regression models
12. Apply these foundations to worked problems in forestry, carbon, and ecology

## The running example {.unnumbered}

To keep the concepts concrete, every chapter draws on one running dataset: a forest inventory from a coastal temperate-rainforest study area in the Alberni Valley on Vancouver Island, British Columbia. The choice of a forestry dataset is incidental to the methods, which are general to all spatial data, but it gives the examples a consistent real-world grounding and lets the applied exercises connect to problems in forest inventory, carbon, and ecology. We work with 140 plots and the individual-tree measurements behind them, alongside terrain, imagery, and administrative layers pulled from open sources.

::: callout-note
## Inventory data

The plot and tree measurements used throughout this book are **simulated** for teaching. They are generated from realistic coastal-BC species compositions and biomass–diameter relationships so that the numbers behave like a real cruise, but they do not describe any specific stand or landowner. Terrain, imagery, coastline, and place-name layers, by contrast, are real and are downloaded from open services (elevation, MODIS/Sentinel imagery, and OpenStreetMap) at render time. Wherever a layer is real, the code shows exactly where it comes from.
:::

## Using this book {.unnumbered}

Each chapter is a self-contained lesson that builds on the last. Code is shown in full and is meant to be run; the fastest way to learn is to work through it line by line and then attempt the exercises at the end of each chapter. The material is pitched at analysts who know some statistics but may be new to R and to GIS.

## Further reading {.unnumbered}

This course covers the standard curriculum of spatial analysis in R, and readers who want a deeper or more general treatment are well served by the canonical texts in the field: @pebesma2023sds, @lovelace2019geocomputation, and @bivand2013asdar. The `sf` and `terra` packages that underpin the modern R-spatial stack are described by @pebesma2018sf and @hijmans2024terra. Where the applied exercises touch forest-carbon conventions, they follow @ipcc2006gl, with illustrative allometry informed by the Canadian national biomass equations of @ung2008allometric.
